note: In flat Belem; fully wheelchair accessible and FREE for disabled visitors, with an accessible toilet and free loaner manual chairs at the entrance. Entrance ramp is a bit steep (staff assist). A must-see.
Iconic riverside tower; exterior, riverside walkway and compass plaza are flat/step-free, but the INTERIOR is not accessible (9cm threshold + narrow spiral staircases, no lift). View from outside.

Waterfront monument in flat Belem; the plaza and compass-rose mosaic are step-free and reachable. Note: crossing from the tram side involves the infamous stairs-only bridge/tunnel - approach from the accessible side.
Lisbon's oldest quarter: a maze of steep, narrow cobbled streets and endless stairs up toward the castle. Genuinely hostile to wheelchairs and hard with a stroller. Atmospheric on foot/carrier only.
Hilltop castle over the city; the bus up is not accessible, but there is a lift partway up near the hospital that gets you close. Grounds have cobbles and slopes. Reached via steep Alfama.
The iconic vintage yellow tram is NOT wheelchair or stroller accessible - steep steps, narrow doors, and long queues. For an accessible tram use the modern low-floor 15E to Belem instead.
The flat downtown grid (Praca do Comercio, Rua Augusta); ~90% of curbs are lowered, largely level and navigable - the easy counterpoint to hilly Alfama.
One of the world's great aquariums at Parque das Nacoes; fully wheelchair accessible and explicitly recommended for families with small children. Under-6s often free.
Iconic 1902 iron elevator linking Baixa to Carmo; the lift itself + viewing platform have access limits and long queues - often best viewed from outside. Free with some transit passes.
Modern flat riverside district (Expo '98 site) with wide promenades, cable car, and the Oceanario; easy, level and stroller/wheelchair friendly.
Not a sight but a hazard: to reach Belem Tower/Discoveries from the tram side, the only pedestrian bridge and tunnel are STAIRS-ONLY. Use an adapted taxi to drop on the waterfront side.
